facts.text: The Grand Ole Opry, founded in 1925, is the longest-running live radio program in the world and remains the heart of country music.
Johnny Cash proposed to June Carter on stage during a live performance in London, Ontario, on February 22, 1968.
The Martin D-28 acoustic guitar has been a staple of country music since the 1930s and was favored by Hank Williams, Johnny Cash, and many others.
Garth Brooks is the best-selling solo artist in U.S. history, surpassing even Elvis Presley with over 157 million domestic albums sold.
Dolly Parton wrote 'Jolene' and 'I Will Always Love You' on the same day.
The Bristol Sessions of 1927 are widely regarded as the 'Big Bang' of country music, recording Jimmie Rodgers and the Carter Family.
Pedal steel guitar, one of country music's most distinctive instruments, evolved from the Hawaiian lap steel guitar in the 1950s.
Willie Nelson didn't achieve mainstream success as a performer until he was over 40 years old, despite writing hits for others for decades.
The Ryman Auditorium in Nashville, known as the 'Mother Church of Country Music,' was originally built as a tabernacle in 1892.
Shania Twain's 'Come On Over' (1997) is the best-selling studio album by a female artist in any genre, with over 40 million copies sold worldwide.
Taylor Swift began her career as a country artist and released her self-titled debut country album in 2006 at just 16 years old.
The Gibson J-45 acoustic guitar, nicknamed 'The Workhorse,' has been a favorite of country artists since its introduction in 1942.
Hank Williams died at age 29 on January 1, 1953, in the back seat of his Cadillac while being driven to a concert in Canton, Ohio.
The CMA Awards were first held in 1967 and are often called 'Country Music's Biggest Night.'
Chris Stapleton worked as a Nashville songwriter for over a decade, penning hits for other artists before his solo breakthrough with 'Traveller' in 2015.
The banjo, a core country instrument, has African origins and was brought to America by enslaved people before being adopted into Appalachian music.
Patsy Cline's 'Crazy' was written by a young, unknown Willie Nelson, who sold it to her for $50.
Luke Combs became the first artist to have his first five singles all reach number one on the Billboard Country Airplay chart.
Nashville's Music Row is a neighborhood southwest of downtown that houses most of the city's major record labels, publishing houses, and recording studios.
George Jones's 'He Stopped Loving Her Today' is widely considered the greatest country song of all time by critics and musicians alike.
The fiddle is technically identical to a violin; the difference is entirely in playing style, with country fiddlers using more slides and double stops.
Carrie Underwood won American Idol in 2005 and has since become one of the most successful country artists ever, with over 85 million records sold.
The Bakersfield Sound emerged in the 1950s and 60s as a raw, electric alternative to the polished Nashville Sound, led by Buck Owens and Merle Haggard.
Waylon Jennings gave up his seat on the plane that crashed and killed Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and the Big Bopper on February 3, 1959.
Garth Brooks's 'Friends in Low Places' was released in 1990 and became an instant anthem, spending four weeks at number one.
Morgan Wallen's 'Dangerous: The Double Album' spent ten consecutive weeks at number one on the Billboard 200 in 2021.
The dobro, or resonator guitar, produces its distinctive sound from aluminum cones inside the body rather than from a traditional soundboard.
Loretta Lynn was one of 8 children raised in a coal miner's cabin in Butcher Hollow, Kentucky, and married at age 15.
RCA Studio B in Nashville, built in 1957, is where Elvis Presley, Dolly Parton, and many country legends recorded some of their biggest hits.
Conway Twitty holds the record for the most number-one country singles, with 55 chart-toppers during his career.
The ACM Awards (Academy of Country Music) began in 1966 in Los Angeles and traditionally take place in a different city than the CMAs.
Johnny Cash's 'Ring of Fire' was co-written by June Carter Cash and Merle Kilgore, inspired by June's conflicted feelings about falling in love with Cash.
Zach Bryan self-released his debut album 'DeAnn' while serving in the U.S. Navy and became a viral sensation without any label support.
The Gibson F-5 mandolin, designed by Lloyd Loar in the 1920s, is considered the gold standard for mandolins and commands six-figure prices for vintage models.
Line dancing became a massive cultural phenomenon in the 1990s, driven largely by Billy Ray Cyrus's 'Achy Breaky Heart.'
Merle Haggard served time at San Quentin Prison, where he saw Johnny Cash perform in 1958, an experience that inspired him to pursue music.
The Nashville Sound of the late 1950s and 60s replaced fiddles and steel guitars with string sections and background vocals to attract pop audiences.
Tim McGraw and Faith Hill, country music's most famous couple, met at a Nashville event in 1994 and married in 1996.
Kris Kristofferson was a Rhodes Scholar and Army helicopter pilot before becoming a Nashville songwriter, landing a job as a janitor at Columbia Recording Studios.
Miranda Lambert has won more ACM Awards than any other artist in history, male or female.
The Deering Banjo Company, founded in 1975 in San Diego, is the largest American banjo manufacturer and produces instruments used across country and bluegrass.
Tammy Wynette's 'Stand by Your Man' became both a country anthem and a feminist lightning rod when it was released in 1968.
Keith Urban, born in New Zealand and raised in Australia, moved to Nashville in 1992 and became one of country's biggest international stars.
Stagecoach Festival in Indio, California, is the largest country music festival in the world, held annually on the same grounds as Coachella.
Dolly Parton has written over 3,000 songs during her career, making her one of the most prolific songwriters in any genre.
David Allan Coe wrote 'Would You Lay with Me (In a Field of Stone)' for Tanya Tucker, which became a number-one hit in 1974.
The upright bass (standup bass) was the standard bass instrument in country music before the electric bass guitar gained popularity in the 1960s.
Florida Georgia Line's 'Cruise' became the first country song to receive a Diamond certification, selling over 10 million copies.
Lainey Wilson was named CMA Entertainer of the Year in 2023 after living in a camper trailer outside Nashville for years while trying to break into the industry.
George Strait holds the record for the most number-one hits on the Billboard Hot Country Songs chart, with 44 chart-toppers over his career.
The Nashville Songwriters Hall of Fame was established in 1970 to honor the craft behind country music's greatest songs.
Johnny Cash wore black as a symbol of solidarity with the poor and suffering, which earned him the nickname 'The Man in Black.'
Sam Hunt's 'Body Like a Back Road' spent 34 weeks in the top spot on the Hot Country Songs chart in 2017, a record at the time.
The harmonica has been a staple of country music since its earliest days, used by artists from Jimmie Rodgers to Charlie McCoy.
Blake Shelton joined The Voice as a coach in its first season in 2011 and stayed for 23 seasons, winning multiple times.
The 'bro-country' era of the early 2010s emphasized party themes with trucks, tailgates, and cold beer, drawing both fans and critics.
Hank Williams Jr. nearly died in a mountain climbing accident in 1975, fracturing his skull and requiring multiple facial reconstruction surgeries.
Willie Nelson's guitar 'Trigger' is a Martin N-20 classical guitar with a gaping hole worn through the top from decades of playing.
Garth Brooks retired from music in 2000 to raise his daughters, returning to touring in 2014 with a sold-out world tour.
The Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum in Nashville opened in 1967 and houses over 2.5 million artifacts.
Carrie Underwood's 'Before He Cheats' won two Grammy Awards and became one of the best-selling country singles in digital history.
Taylor Swift's 'Fearless' won Album of the Year at the 2010 Grammys, making her the youngest artist to win that award at 20.
Pedal steel guitar requires both hands, both knees, and both feet to operate, making it one of the most physically demanding instruments to play.
Luke Bryan has hosted the CMA Awards multiple times and was also a judge on American Idol starting in 2018.
CMA Fest (formerly Fan Fair) in Nashville draws over 80,000 country music fans each June for concerts, autograph sessions, and events.
Patsy Cline died in a plane crash on March 5, 1963, at just 30 years old, along with fellow Opry stars Cowboy Copas and Hawkshaw Hawkins.
The Taylor guitar company, founded in 1974, became a favorite among modern country artists for its bright, projecting tone.
Johnny Cash recorded live albums at both Folsom Prison (1968) and San Quentin Prison (1969), earning him legendary status among inmates and fans alike.
Chris Stapleton has written over 1,000 songs, including hits recorded by Adele, Luke Bryan, and Dierks Bentley before his solo career took off.
The honky-tonk era of the 1940s and 50s brought electrified instruments and raw, emotional lyrics to country music, with stars like Hank Williams leading the way.
Dolly Parton turned down Elvis Presley's request to record 'I Will Always Love You' because his manager demanded half the publishing rights.
Jason Aldean's 'Try That in a Small Town' sparked significant cultural debate in 2023 and topped multiple charts despite — or because of — the controversy.
The Gibson RB series of banjos, particularly the RB-250 and RB-800, have been workhorses of country and bluegrass music for decades.
Loretta Lynn's autobiography 'Coal Miner's Daughter' was adapted into an Oscar-winning 1980 film starring Sissy Spacek.
The New Traditionalist movement of the 1980s, led by George Strait, Randy Travis, and Reba McEntire, returned country to its roots after the Urban Cowboy pop era.
Willie Nelson hosted the first Farm Aid concert on September 22, 1985, to raise money for American family farmers.
Luke Combs's debut album 'This One's for You' was certified Diamond, a rare achievement in any genre of music.
Country radio remains one of the most listened-to formats in America, consistently ranking in the top three by audience share.
Shania Twain contracted Lyme disease and lost her singing voice for years before making a comeback in 2017.
Garth Brooks's 'The Dance' was released as a single in 1990 and is frequently cited as one of the greatest country songs ever written.
Merle Haggard wrote 'Okie from Muskogee' as a tribute to his father and working-class Americans during the turbulent late 1960s.
The CMT Music Awards are fan-voted, making them unique among the major country music award shows.
Zach Bryan's album 'Zach Bryan' debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 in 2023, a rarity for an independent country artist.
The cowboy hat became synonymous with country music in the 1940s and 50s, though many early country artists wore suits and fedoras.
Conway Twitty started his career as a rock and roll artist with the hit 'It's Only Make Believe' before transitioning fully to country music.
Waylon Jennings and Willie Nelson's 'Wanted! The Outlaws' (1976) was the first country album to be certified platinum.
The Grand Ole Opry moved from the Ryman Auditorium to the Grand Ole Opry House in 1974, though it still holds special shows at the Ryman.
Faith Hill's 'Breathe' (1999) crossed over to pop, reaching number one on the Billboard Hot 100 and selling over 8 million copies.
The Nashville Songwriters Association International (NSAI) was founded in 1967 and is the world's largest not-for-profit songwriter advocacy organization.
Morgan Wallen's 'Last Night' spent 16 weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100, setting a record for a country song on the pop chart.
Dolly Parton was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2022, initially declining the nomination before accepting.
The pedal steel guitar was pioneered by Bud Isaacs, whose 1956 recording of 'Slowly' introduced the pedal mechanism to country music.
George Jones was notorious for his hard-drinking lifestyle and once drove a riding lawn mower to a liquor store when his wife hid the car keys.
Lainey Wilson wrote and performed songs for the TV show Yellowstone, which helped boost her career to mainstream success.
The Carter Family — A.P., Sara, and Maybelle — are considered the 'First Family of Country Music' and recorded at the Bristol Sessions in 1927.
Keith Urban is known for his electric guitar virtuosity, unusual in mainstream country, and often shreds solos during his live shows.
Johnny Cash's 'Folsom Prison Blues' was inspired by a documentary about Folsom Prison that he saw while serving in the Air Force in Germany.
Bonnaroo festival in Manchester, Tennessee, has increasingly featured country and Americana acts alongside its rock and indie lineup.
Tim McGraw's 'Live Like You Were Dying' was inspired by his father, former MLB pitcher Tug McGraw, who died of brain cancer in 2004.
The 'Nashville Sound' was developed by producers Chet Atkins and Owen Bradley to make country music more commercially appealing to urban audiences.
Miranda Lambert's 'The House That Built Me' was originally turned down by multiple artists before she recorded it and took it to number one.
Hank Williams was fired from the Grand Ole Opry in 1952 for chronic drunkenness and unreliability, just months before his death.
Country music's truck culture is so pervasive that pickup trucks appear in the lyrics of roughly one in six country hits.
Kris Kristofferson landed a helicopter on Johnny Cash's lawn to deliver him a demo tape, which led to Cash recording 'Sunday Mornin' Comin' Down.'
The first Grammy for Best Country Recording was awarded in 1959 to the Kingston Trio for 'Tom Dooley.'
Blake Shelton's 'Austin' was his debut single and spent five weeks at number one in 2001.
Garth Brooks sold out nine shows at Croke Park in Dublin, Ireland, in 2014 but had to cancel all five when the city wouldn't approve all dates.
The Telecaster guitar, introduced by Fender in 1950, became the quintessential electric country guitar due to its bright, twangy tone.
Tammy Wynette and George Jones's tumultuous marriage and musical collaborations made them country's most legendary couple.
Carrie Underwood's 'Blown Away' album tour grossed over $50 million, making it one of the highest-grossing country tours of 2012-2013.
The Outlaw country movement of the 1970s was a rebellion against the polished Nashville Sound, embracing raw, unfiltered recording styles.
Willie Nelson was born on April 29, 1933, in Abbott, Texas, and learned guitar from his grandfather.
Sam Hunt was a college football quarterback before pursuing music, playing at Middle Tennessee State University and the University of Alabama at Birmingham.
The Country Music Association (CMA) was founded in 1958 as the first trade organization to promote a specific genre of music.
Dolly Parton's Dollywood theme park in Pigeon Forge, Tennessee, attracts over 3 million visitors annually and is a major employer in the region.
Luke Bryan's 'Crash My Party' tour in 2014 was the top-grossing country tour of the year.
The dobro takes its name from the Dopyera Brothers (Do-Bro) who invented the resonator guitar in the late 1920s.
George Strait's 1992 film 'Pure Country' was a box office disappointment but its soundtrack became one of the best-selling country albums of the year.
Johnny Cash was born on February 26, 1932, in Kingsland, Arkansas.
Chris Stapleton won three CMA Awards on the same night in 2015 — Male Vocalist, New Artist, and Album of the Year — launching his solo career.
The steel guitar was introduced to country music in the 1930s, borrowed from Hawaiian music, which was hugely popular in America at the time.
Florida Georgia Line formed in 2010 when Tyler Hubbard and Brian Kelley met at Belmont University in Nashville.
Merle Haggard was born on April 6, 1937, in a converted railroad boxcar in Bakersfield, California.
Loretta Lynn's 'The Pill' (1975) was banned by many country radio stations for its frank discussion of birth control.
The Grand Ole Opry stage features a circle of wood from the original Ryman Auditorium stage, on which performers stand as a tradition.
Zach Bryan served in the U.S. Navy for eight years before receiving an honorable discharge to pursue music full-time.
Shania Twain's 'Man! I Feel Like a Woman!' became a karaoke and pop culture staple and crossed over to mainstream pop radio.
Hank Williams wrote 'Your Cheatin' Heart' during a car ride, dictating the lyrics to his fiancée Billie Jean Jones.
Patsy Cline survived a near-fatal car accident in 1961, just two years before her death in a plane crash, and recorded 'Crazy' while still recovering.
Country music's roots combine Appalachian folk, blues, gospel, and Celtic traditional music brought by Scots-Irish immigrants.
Jason Aldean's 'Night Train' album was certified triple-platinum and produced four number-one singles.
The Nashville Network (TNN) launched in 1983 as the first cable channel devoted to country music and country lifestyle programming.
Garth Brooks performed at Joe Biden's inauguration on January 20, 2021, singing 'Amazing Grace' a cappella.
Morgan Wallen was initially eliminated from The Voice in 2014 but was saved by Usher's team before ultimately being voted off.
The five-string banjo, played with finger picks in the 'three-finger' Scruggs style, became central to country and bluegrass through Earl Scruggs's innovations.
Keith Urban proposed to Nicole Kidman with a diamond ring that cost a reported $64,000, and they married in 2006.
Conway Twitty changed his stage name from Harold Jenkins, combining the names of Conway, Arkansas, and Twitty, Texas.
Lainey Wilson grew up in Baskin, Louisiana, a tiny town with a population of about 250 people.
Dolly Parton wrote 'Jolene' about a bank teller who flirted with her husband Carl Dean.
The ACM Entertainer of the Year award has been won by Garth Brooks a record seven times.
Waylon Jennings recorded the narration for the TV show 'The Dukes of Hazzard' and sang the theme song 'Good Ol' Boys.'
Country music made up approximately 10% of total U.S. music consumption in 2023, making it consistently one of the top three genres.
Tim McGraw has sold more than 80 million records worldwide and had 10 consecutive albums debut at number one.
The Ryman Auditorium has a seating capacity of about 2,362 and is known for its exceptional acoustics, originally designed to project sermons.
Miranda Lambert and Blake Shelton's high-profile divorce in 2015 was one of the most publicized events in country music history.
The term 'honky-tonk' originally referred to rowdy bars in the American South and Southwest where early country musicians performed.
Willie Nelson's 'Stardust' album (1978) featured pop standards instead of country songs and stayed on the charts for over 10 years.
Carrie Underwood is a vegan and an advocate for animal rights, which is unusual among mainstream country artists.
George Jones earned the nickname 'The Possum' from his music manager who thought his haircut made him resemble the animal.
Luke Combs married Nicole Hocking in 2020 after the couple met at a music festival in 2016.
Johnny Cash recorded 'Hurt,' a Nine Inch Nails cover, at age 70, and its music video is considered one of the greatest ever made.
The mandolin was brought into country music from Italian and Appalachian folk traditions and featured prominently in Western swing.
Hank Williams Jr.'s 'All My Rowdy Friends Are Coming Over Tonight' became the long-running Monday Night Football theme song.
David Allan Coe claims to have spent 20 years in various reform schools and prisons before beginning his music career.
Dolly Parton was born on January 19, 1946, in a one-room cabin in Locust Ridge, Tennessee, one of 12 children.
The 'Nashville Number System' is a shorthand method of writing chord progressions that allows session musicians to play songs in any key on the spot.
Blake Shelton and Gwen Stefani began dating in 2015 after both serving as coaches on The Voice, and married in 2021.
Chris Stapleton's 'Tennessee Whiskey' was originally recorded by David Allan Coe in 1981 and later George Jones before Stapleton made it iconic.
Stagecoach Festival was first held in 2007 and regularly draws over 75,000 attendees across its three-day run.
The first country music recordings were made in the early 1920s by artists like Fiddlin' John Carson and Eck Robertson.
Garth Brooks performed under the rock alias 'Chris Gaines' in 1999, releasing an album that confused fans but sold two million copies.
Tammy Wynette was known as the 'First Lady of Country Music' and had 20 number-one hits during her career.
Faith Hill was adopted as an infant and grew up in Star, Mississippi, a town with a population of about 500.
Willie Nelson's annual Fourth of July Picnic has been held since 1973 and is one of country music's most beloved traditions.
Zach Bryan's 'Something in the Orange' went viral on TikTok and reached the top 10 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The resonator guitar was originally designed to be louder than traditional acoustic guitars for playing in noisy dance halls before amplification.
Loretta Lynn was the first woman to be named CMA Entertainer of the Year, winning in 1972.
Country music radio stations numbered over 2,000 in the United States by the early 2020s, more than any other format.
Morgan Wallen's mullet hairstyle became a signature look that inspired fans to adopt the retro style at concerts.
The 'Urban Cowboy' film (1980) starring John Travolta triggered a massive mainstream crossover for country music and a mechanical bull craze.
Merle Haggard received a full pardon from California Governor Ronald Reagan in 1972 for his earlier criminal convictions.
Lainey Wilson was named CMA Female Vocalist of the Year in both 2023 and 2024, back-to-back.
Johnny Cash's 'I Walk the Line' was his first number-one hit and spent six weeks at the top in 1956.
The Martin D-28 was the guitar used by Hank Williams on nearly all of his recordings, and his original instrument is in the Country Music Hall of Fame.
Shania Twain's music combined country with pop and rock elements, essentially creating the 'pop-country' genre in the 1990s.
Keith Urban has won four Grammy Awards and played a pivotal role in bringing Australian and rock influences into mainstream country.
The term 'country and western' was the official genre name used by the music industry until the CMA dropped 'western' in the 1960s.
Jason Aldean's 2017 concert in Las Vegas was the site of the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history, killing 60 people.
Sam Hunt's 'Montevallo' album was named after his wife Hannah Lee Fowler's hometown in Alabama.
The Grand Ole Opry has a strict rule that performers must play the Opry at least once a year to maintain their membership.
Dolly Parton started the Imagination Library in 1995, which has since given away over 200 million free books to children worldwide.
Tim McGraw's father, Tug McGraw, was a famous MLB relief pitcher who didn't acknowledge Tim as his son until Tim was 18.
The Bakersfield Sound's raw, electric guitar-driven style directly influenced the Outlaw country movement a decade later.
Carrie Underwood became only the seventh member of the Grand Ole Opry to be inducted by a current U.S. president when she was inducted in 2008.
Luke Bryan grew up in Leesburg, Georgia, and experienced family tragedy when his older brother Chris died in a car accident in 1996.
The Gibson J-200, known as the 'King of the Flat-tops,' has been played by everyone from Elvis Presley to Emmylou Harris in country music.
Miranda Lambert opened Casa Rosa, a bar-restaurant on Broadway in Nashville, joining a trend of country stars with downtown venues.
Waylon Jennings recorded his autobiography's audiobook before his death in 2002, providing a firsthand account of the Outlaw country era.
George Strait's farewell tour in 2014 set an attendance record with over 104,000 fans at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
Country music has traditionally been strongest in the American South and Midwest, but its fan base has grown significantly in the Northeast and West Coast.
Hank Williams was inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame in 1961, one of its inaugural three members.
Chris Stapleton and his wife Morgane co-wrote several of his songs, and she sings harmony on most of his recordings and live performances.
The pedal steel guitar typically has 10 strings and up to 8 pedals and 4 or more knee levers to change pitches.
Florida Georgia Line's 'Meant to Be' featuring Bebe Rexha spent 50 weeks in the top 10 of the Billboard Hot 100.
Conway Twitty and Loretta Lynn recorded a series of hit duets in the 1970s, including 'After the Fire Is Gone' and 'Louisiana Woman, Mississippi Man.'
Zach Bryan won a Grammy for Best Country Song for 'I Remember Everything' featuring Kacey Musgraves.
The 'beer culture' theme in country music accelerated in the 2010s, with brands like Bud Light and Coors sponsoring major tours and festivals.
Patsy Cline's 'Walkin' After Midnight' was her first hit, released in 1957 after she performed it on Arthur Godfrey's Talent Scouts.
Willie Nelson has released over 100 studio albums during his career, more than almost any other major artist in any genre.
Luke Combs's song 'Fast Car' was a cover of Tracy Chapman's 1988 original, and it brought new attention to Chapman's songwriting legacy.
The Country Music Hall of Fame currently has over 150 inducted members, including performers, songwriters, and industry executives.
Garth Brooks's 'Ropin' the Wind' was the first country album to debut at number one on the Billboard 200 in 1991.
Dolly Parton's '9 to 5' was written while she was filming the movie of the same name, and she clicked her acrylic fingernails to keep rhythm.
Morgan Wallen's 'One Thing at a Time' album debuted with 259,000 album-equivalent units in its first week in 2023.
The tradition of cowboy boots in country music dates back to the singing cowboys of 1930s and 40s cinema like Gene Autry and Roy Rogers.
Loretta Lynn died on October 4, 2022, at her home in Hurricane Mills, Tennessee, at age 90.
Keith Urban practices guitar for several hours daily and is regarded as one of the most technically skilled guitarists in country music.
The New Traditionalist movement was partially a reaction to the pop-leaning crossover hits of the early 1980s 'Urban Cowboy' era.
Blake Shelton's 'God Gave Me You' became a popular wedding song and reached number one on the country charts in 2011.
Kris Kristofferson wrote 'Me and Bobby McGee,' which became a number-one posthumous hit for Janis Joplin in 1971.
Merle Haggard earned the nickname 'The Poet of the Common Man' for his songs about working-class life and personal struggle.
The CMA Awards were broadcast on television for the first time in 1968 on NBC.
Lainey Wilson's hit 'Watermelon Moonshine' is an autobiographical song about growing up in rural Louisiana.
The acoustic guitar remains the most fundamental instrument in country music, used by virtually every artist from beginners to legends.
Tammy Wynette's 'D-I-V-O-R-C-E' was written to be the opposite of her hit 'Stand by Your Man' and also reached number one.
Sam Hunt pioneered a 'country-R&B' sound that influenced a wave of genre-blending artists in the mid-2010s.
Johnny Cash hosted his own television variety show, 'The Johnny Cash Show,' from 1969 to 1971 on ABC.
Taylor Swift's transition from country to pop with 'Red' (2012) and fully with '1989' (2014) was one of the most successful genre crossovers in music history.
The banjo typically has five strings, with the short fifth string (drone string) being a distinctive feature that creates its characteristic sound.
George Jones and Tammy Wynette divorced in 1975 but continued to record together professionally for years afterward.
Garth Brooks has sold over 170 million albums worldwide, making him one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
Chris Stapleton's 'Traveller' was recorded largely live in the studio at RCA Studio A in Nashville with minimal overdubs.
The Western swing genre, pioneered by Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys in the 1930s, blended country with jazz, blues, and big band music.
Miranda Lambert co-wrote 'Tin Man' with Jack Ingram and Jon Randall, drawing on personal heartbreak from her divorce.
The 'Nashville cat' is slang for the elite session musicians who play on the majority of country recordings made in Nashville.
Hank Williams's 'I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry' was later covered by Elvis Presley, who called it the saddest song he'd ever heard.
Carrie Underwood suffered a serious fall outside her home in 2017 that required over 40 stitches in her face.
Willie Nelson became one of the most prominent celebrity advocates for marijuana legalization and even launched his own brand, Willie's Reserve.
The 'A-Team' was the nickname given to Nashville's top session musicians in the 1950s-70s, including guitarist Grady Martin and pianist Floyd Cramer.
Luke Combs performed at the NFL Draft in Nashville in 2019, helping highlight the city's country music identity to a broader audience.
Dolly Parton refused the Presidential Medal of Freedom twice — once from Donald Trump and once from Joe Biden.
Morgan Wallen grew up in Sneedville, Tennessee, a small town in the Appalachian region, and played baseball and basketball in high school.
The Opry Mills shopping mall adjacent to the Grand Ole Opry House was built on the site of the former Opryland USA theme park.
David Allan Coe's 'You Never Even Called Me by My Name' is often jokingly called 'the perfect country song' for including all the genre's clichés.
Hank Williams Jr. has had a career spanning over 60 years, with his own hits and his father's legendary shadow.
The fiddle and violin are the same instrument; in country music, 'fiddle' is used when the instrument is played with slides, drones, and double stops.
Keith Urban won his first CMA Award for Male Vocalist of the Year in 2004.
Zach Bryan released his critically acclaimed album 'American Heartbreak' as a triple album with 34 tracks in 2022.
Country music's influence on rock and roll is profound — Elvis Presley, Jerry Lee Lewis, and Carl Perkins all had deep country roots.
Jason Aldean holds the record for the most consecutive number-one hits on the Billboard Country Airplay chart, with 7 in a row.
The Gibson Hummingbird guitar, introduced in 1960, became a popular choice for country artists wanting a warm, rich tone.
Blake Shelton released his album 'Based on a True Story' in 2013, which was certified four times platinum.
Loretta Lynn wrote 'Coal Miner's Daughter' in five minutes while sitting on her tour bus, drawing from childhood memories.
The 'Rhinestone Cowboy' aesthetic of bedazzled suits in country music was popularized by Nudie Cohn, who designed outfits for Hank Williams and Johnny Cash.
Tim McGraw and Faith Hill's 'Soul2Soul' tours were among the highest-grossing concert tours by any country act.
Patsy Cline was posthumously awarded a Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award in 1995.
Luke Bryan's 'Country Girl (Shake It for Me)' epitomized the bro-country era with its party-focused lyrics and high-energy video.
The first CMA Entertainer of the Year was Eddy Arnold, who won the inaugural award in 1967.
Johnny Cash and Bob Dylan were close friends and recorded together, including the duet 'Girl from the North Country' on Dylan's Nashville Skyline album.
Shania Twain grew up in extreme poverty in Timmins, Ontario, Canada, sometimes going without food as a child.
Lainey Wilson's song 'Things a Man Oughta Know' was her breakthrough hit and won CMA Song of the Year in 2022.
The Grand Ole Opry broadcasts on WSM radio, which has been on air since 1925 with the call letters standing for 'We Shield Millions.'
Waylon Jennings's 'Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way' was a direct critique of the Nashville music establishment.
George Strait is known as the 'King of Country' and never changed his traditional style despite shifting musical trends.
The steel guitar family includes lap steel, pedal steel, and console steel, each with different playing techniques and sounds.
Garth Brooks was the first artist to have seven albums each achieve Diamond status (10 million or more in sales) by the RIAA.
Dolly Parton spent $500 million of her own fortune on the Imagination Library before it received significant outside funding.
Chris Stapleton was the lead vocalist of the bluegrass band The SteelDrivers before going solo.
The 'country twang' vocal style involves a nasal quality produced by singing with a higher soft palate and through the nose slightly.
Luke Combs is known for his everyman persona and has spoken openly about his struggles with anxiety and OCD.
Merle Haggard's 'Mama Tried' was autobiographical, reflecting his difficult relationship with his mother during his troubled youth.
Miranda Lambert opened MuttNation Foundation in 2009 to promote the adoption of shelter animals, a cause close to her heart.
The 'Nashville Sound' was sometimes called 'countrypolitan' for its blend of country sensibilities with metropolitan pop production values.
Music Row in Nashville stretches along 16th and 17th Avenues South and has been the center of country music business since the 1950s.
Conway Twitty's real estate ventures included building Twitty City, a tourist attraction in Hendersonville, Tennessee, near his home.
Willie Nelson celebrated his 90th birthday with a star-studded concert at the Hollywood Bowl on April 29, 2023.
Hank Williams was inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame in 1970, recognized for his profound influence on American popular music.
Morgan Wallen named his son Indigo Wilder Wallen, born in July 2020.
Taylor Swift's 'Mean' won two Grammy Awards in 2012 and was written as a response to a music critic who gave her a harsh review.
The country two-step dance originated in the 19th century and remains one of the most popular partner dances at country venues.
Carrie Underwood has won seven CMA Awards, seven ACM Awards, and 15 Grammy nominations throughout her career.
The Telecaster's 'chicken pickin'' technique, using hybrid picking to create staccato, clucking tones, is a signature sound of country lead guitar.
Zach Bryan filmed the music video for 'Heading South' on his iPhone while still deployed in the Navy.
Loretta Lynn and Patsy Cline were close friends, and Cline's death in 1963 deeply affected Lynn for the rest of her life.
Keith Urban was previously a member of the country band The Ranch, which released one album before he went solo.
The CMA's Song of the Year award recognizes the songwriter rather than the performing artist, honoring the craft of songwriting.
Jason Aldean's 'Dirt Road Anthem' blended country with hip-hop-influenced talk-singing, pioneering the country-rap crossover.
Tammy Wynette earned over $1 million annually at her peak but was often embroiled in financial difficulties and personal turmoil.
Johnny Cash was one of the first major American musicians to perform a concert in a prison, paving the way for prison concert programs.
The 'Opry Circle' — the six-foot circle of oak on the Grand Ole Opry stage — is considered hallowed ground by country musicians.
Garth Brooks and Trisha Yearwood, two of country music's biggest stars, married in 2005 after years of friendship.
George Jones once went five years between studio albums in the early 1980s due to drug and alcohol problems.
The Nudie suit, a bedazzled Western outfit, was created by tailor Nudie Cohn and became a country music fashion icon in the 1950s and 60s.
Dolly Parton has been married to Carl Dean since 1966, and he has almost never appeared in public or at her events.
Tim McGraw has acted in several films, including 'Friday Night Lights,' 'The Blind Side,' and '1883.'
Kris Kristofferson's 'Help Me Make It Through the Night' was controversial at the time for its frank depiction of physical intimacy.
The 'murder ballad' is a traditional country and folk storytelling form that recounts tales of crime and violence, dating back centuries.
Blake Shelton's version of 'Ol' Red' tells the story of a prisoner using a dog to plan an escape, originally recorded by George Jones.
Luke Bryan performed the national anthem at Super Bowl LI in Houston, Texas, in 2017.
Faith Hill's original name is Audrey Faith Perry. She took the name 'Hill' after moving to Nashville to pursue music.
The country genre has influenced hip-hop more than many realize — artists like Nelly, Lil Nas X, and Post Malone have incorporated country elements.
Sam Hunt's debut album 'Montevallo' was certified platinum within its first year and produced four top-five singles.
Merle Haggard released 38 number-one country singles during his career, spanning from the 1960s to the 2000s.
Miranda Lambert won CMA Female Vocalist of the Year seven consecutive times from 2010 to 2016, an unprecedented streak.
The origins of 'yodeling' in country music come from Swiss and Central European immigrants who settled in Appalachia.
Lainey Wilson's 'Bell Bottom Country' genre description is a nod to the retro-influenced style she brings to modern country.
David Allan Coe reportedly lived in a hearse for several years before finding success in Nashville.
Shania Twain's comeback album 'Now' in 2017 debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 after a 15-year gap between studio albums.
Country music's 'three chords and the truth' philosophy, attributed to Harlan Howard, summarizes the genre's emphasis on honest storytelling.
Chris Stapleton's vocal range spans from deep baritone to soaring falsetto, making him one of the most versatile vocalists in country.
The Gibson RB-250 banjo has been the most popular first professional banjo for country and bluegrass players since the 1960s.
Morgan Wallen's 'Wasted on You' was written during the COVID-19 lockdown and became a number-one hit on multiple charts.
Hank Williams's 'I Saw the Light' was inspired by the gospel music he heard at his local church in Georgiana, Alabama.
Willie Nelson has won 13 Grammy Awards spanning categories from country to pop to Americana.
Carrie Underwood holds the record for the most wins at the CMT Music Awards, with 25 total victories.
The twin fiddle technique, featuring two fiddlers playing harmony parts simultaneously, is a signature element of Western swing and Texas country.
Garth Brooks refused to release his music on iTunes for years, holding out until 2014 when he launched his own streaming service, GhostTunes.
Luke Combs's 'Beer Never Broke My Heart' tour in 2019 sold out arenas across North America.
George Jones died on April 26, 2013, with his funeral held at the Grand Ole Opry House and attended by thousands.
The 'Outlaw' name came from a 1973 Lee Clayton song referencing Waylon Jennings, and it stuck as a genre label.
Johnny Cash proposed to June Carter Cash live on stage at a concert in London, Ontario, on February 22, 1968, and she said yes.
Keith Urban opened a bar and music venue called 'Keith Urban's Bar' in Nashville, joining the growing Broadway scene.
Loretta Lynn's feud with Patsy Cline was entirely fictional — the two were best friends who supported each other's careers.
The Fender Telecaster was originally called the Broadcaster when it was released in 1950, but a trademark dispute forced the name change.
Dolly Parton's 'Coat of Many Colors' is based on a true story from her childhood about a coat her mother made from rags.
Tim McGraw was born on May 1, 1967, in Delhi, Louisiana, and raised in Start, Louisiana.
The Grand Ole Opry was broadcast from the stage of the War Memorial Auditorium and several other venues before settling at the Ryman in 1943.
Zach Bryan won the Grammy for Best New Artist nomination in 2024, reflecting the industry's recognition of his rapid rise.
Tammy Wynette recorded 'Stand by Your Man' in just 20 minutes, and it became the best-selling country single by a female artist at the time.
Blake Shelton's career spans over 20 years, with 28 number-one country singles and multiple platinum albums.
The tradition of 'porch picking' — informal jam sessions on front porches — remains a core part of country and bluegrass culture.
Miranda Lambert's 'Gunpowder & Lead' was inspired by a real domestic violence case and became a powerful anthem.
Country music fashion has evolved from Nudie suits and cowboy hats to include trucker caps, flannel shirts, and modern Western wear.
Luke Bryan has sold over 75 million records worldwide and is one of the most streamed country artists on Spotify.
Waylon Jennings died on February 13, 2002, in Chandler, Arizona, from complications of diabetes.
The 12-string acoustic guitar has been used in country music for its rich, shimmering sound, particularly in ballads and folk-influenced songs.
George Strait has never performed an encore at any of his concerts, preferring to leave the audience wanting more.
Shania Twain was raised by her stepfather after her parents divorced, and she credits him with encouraging her musical talents.
The phrase 'hat act' is Nashville slang for a male country singer who wears a cowboy hat, particularly common in the 1990s.
Dolly Parton's 'Islands in the Stream,' a duet with Kenny Rogers written by the Bee Gees, reached number one on the pop charts in 1983.
The Country Music Association was founded on November 1, 1958, to promote and advance the genre's commercial prospects.
Chris Stapleton's 'Starting Over' album debuted at number one during the COVID-19 pandemic and won multiple Grammy nominations.
Merle Haggard and Willie Nelson frequently toured together and their friendship defined much of Outlaw country's spirit.
Jason Aldean's 'My Kinda Party' album spent 193 weeks on the Billboard 200, one of the longest chart runs for a country album.
Johnny Cash died on September 12, 2003, just four months after the death of his beloved wife June Carter Cash.
The lap steel guitar was the precursor to the pedal steel and was played flat on the player's lap or on a stand.
Morgan Wallen grew up listening to classic rock and hip-hop alongside country, which influences his genre-blending sound.
Lainey Wilson's 'Wait in the Truck' featuring Hardy dealt with domestic violence themes and reached the top of the country charts.
The Ernest Tubb Record Shop on Broadway in Nashville has been a destination for country music fans since 1947.
Hank Williams's legacy includes influencing artists as diverse as Bob Dylan, Bruce Springsteen, and Mick Jagger.
Kenny Rogers's 'The Gambler' was written by Don Schlitz, who was rejected by every major artist in Nashville before Rogers recorded it.
Faith Hill and Tim McGraw have three daughters together and are considered country music's most enduring power couple.
The 'train song' is a classic country subgenre, with tracks like Cash's 'Folsom Prison Blues' and Merle Haggard's 'Mama Tried' featuring railroad imagery.
Garth Brooks's 'The Thunder Rolls' was controversial for its domestic violence-themed music video, which was banned by CMT and TNN.
Willie Nelson's signature braids became an iconic look starting in the 1970s, though he was clean-cut earlier in his Nashville career.
Dolly Parton's 'Jolene' has been covered in over 30 languages and by artists from the White Stripes to Miley Cyrus.
The Nashville Songwriters Hall of Fame has inducted over 200 songwriters since its founding in 1970.
Luke Combs grew up in Charlotte, North Carolina, and began performing at open mic nights while attending Appalachian State University.
Conway Twitty earned an estimated $50 million during his career and was one of the highest-paid country performers of his era.
The 'truck song' stereotype in country became so prevalent that a viral comedy skit mocked how every country hit mentions trucks, dirt roads, and beer.
Carrie Underwood's Super Bowl LII national anthem performance in 2018 was watched by over 100 million viewers.
Kris Kristofferson served as a captain in the U.S. Army and was a helicopter pilot before giving up a teaching position at West Point to move to Nashville.
The 'Highwaymen' supergroup featured Johnny Cash, Willie Nelson, Waylon Jennings, and Kris Kristofferson.
George Jones recorded over 160 albums during his career, one of the most prolific outputs in country music history.
The Quonset Hut studio on Music Row was one of Nashville's first professional recording studios and home to many classic country recordings.
Keith Urban released 'Graffiti U' in 2018, which debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit 'Coming Home.'
Taylor Swift was the youngest person to single-handedly write and record a number-one country song with 'Our Song' at age 17.
Blake Shelton was born on June 18, 1976, in Ada, Oklahoma, and moved to Nashville at age 17.
The autoharp, a zither-type instrument, was popular in early country and folk music and was a favorite of the Carter Family.
Zach Bryan's live performances are known for their raw energy and stripped-down arrangements, often featuring just his voice and acoustic guitar.
Sam Hunt was the first male country artist in 22 years to top the Billboard Hot 100 with 'Body Like a Back Road.'
Dolly Parton has received 51 Grammy nominations, making her one of the most nominated female artists in Grammy history.
The term 'Americana' emerged in the 1990s to describe a roots-oriented blend of country, folk, blues, and rock that didn't fit mainstream country radio.
Merle Haggard died on April 6, 2016 — his 79th birthday — at his ranch in Palo Cedro, California.
Garth Brooks's concert in Central Park in 1997 drew an estimated 980,000 people, one of the largest free concerts in history.
The 'talking blues' style used by artists like Hank Williams and Woody Guthrie influenced the spoken-word elements found in modern country music.
Reba McEntire has sold over 75 million records and hosted the CMA Awards multiple times, earning the title 'Queen of Country.'
Jimmie Rodgers, the 'Father of Country Music,' died of tuberculosis on May 26, 1933, just two days after his final recording session.
Nashville's Lower Broadway, known as 'Honky Tonk Highway,' features live country music in bars and clubs 365 days a year.
Alan Jackson's 'Where Were You (When the World Stopped Turning)' was written and performed within weeks of the September 11, 2001, attacks.
The capo is one of the most commonly used accessories in country guitar playing, allowing quick key changes to match a singer's vocal range.
Randy Travis's 'Storms of Life' (1986) was the first debut album by a country artist to be certified platinum.
Toby Keith wrote 'Courtesy of the Red, White and Blue' in just 20 minutes after the September 11 attacks, and it became a patriotic anthem.
The Opry introduced a rule that no member could miss more than one Saturday night per year without a valid excuse, later relaxed to accommodate touring schedules.
Brooks & Dunn hold the record for the most CMA Vocal Duo of the Year wins, with 14 victories over their career.
Charley Pride was the first Black artist to achieve major success in country music, earning three CMA Awards in the late 1960s and early 1970s.
The 'boom-chicka-boom' rhythm, a hallmark of Johnny Cash's sound, was created by his Tennessee Two band using a damped guitar string technique.
Eric Church is known for playing three-plus hour marathon concerts and refusing to use a setlist, choosing songs spontaneously based on crowd energy.
Chet Atkins, nicknamed 'Mr. Guitar,' was both a legendary guitarist and a producer who shaped the Nashville Sound for RCA Records.
The first African American woman to perform at the Grand Ole Opry was DeFord Bailey, a harmonica player who became an Opry regular in 1927.
Dierks Bentley's 'Drunk on a Plane' became a party anthem at festivals and exemplified the lighter side of modern country.
The 'countrypolitan' era produced crossover hits by artists like Glen Campbell, whose 'Rhinestone Cowboy' hit number one on both country and pop charts in 1975.
Vince Gill has won 22 Grammy Awards, the most of any male country artist in history.
Buck Owens co-hosted the television show Hee Haw from 1969 to 1986, bringing the Bakersfield Sound to millions of living rooms.
The ShoBud pedal steel guitar company, founded in Nashville in 1956, produced many of the classic pedal steels heard on country recordings.
Clint Black's debut album 'Killin' Time' produced five consecutive number-one singles in 1989-1990, a record for a debut country album at the time.
Tanya Tucker had her first hit 'Delta Dawn' at the age of 13 in 1972, making her one of the youngest country stars ever.
